title: The Day the Sky Shattered
content:
The first thing Ren noticed was the silence.

One moment, he was straddling his electric scooter, phone clamped between his ear and shoulder, a bag of mapo tofu sweating through its plastic container in the delivery basket behind him. The next moment, the engine died. The streetlights flickered once, twice, then went dark. Every car in a three-block radius simply stopped.

And then the sky cracked.

Not like thunder. Not like lightning. Like glass. A sound that started as a whisper and rose to a shriek, a billion fractures spiderwebbing across the blue vault of heaven. People stepped out of shops, out of cars, out of their own front doors, staring up with mouths open and phones raised.

Ren dropped his phone. It hit the pavement and the screen shattered, but he didn't care. He couldn't look away.

The sky fell.

Not in pieces. In a wave. A ripple of nothing that swept from horizon to horizon, and when it passed, the sky was gone. Replaced by a void of swirling colors—purples and greens and golds that hurt to look at. And through that void, Ren could see *other* worlds. Other cities. Other skies. All of them broken, all of them bleeding into each other.

The ground shook.

Ren grabbed his scooter's handlebars to steady himself, but the asphalt beneath his feet was *moving*. Cracking. Splitting. A chasm opened fifty meters down the street, swallowing a convenience store whole. The building to his right groaned, tilted, and then began to rise, lifting free of the earth like a ship breaking from its moorings.

"Fuck," Ren whispered.

A window shattered beside him. Then another. Then every window on the block, blown out by some pressure differential he couldn't understand. He threw his arm up to shield his face, felt glass rain across his jacket, and when he looked again, the world had ended.

Or rather, it had become something else.

The street he'd been standing on—Chengdu's Jinli Old Street, with its red lanterns and tourist shops—was now a floating island. A chunk of earth maybe a kilometer wide, drifting through a sea of impossible colors. Other islands floated nearby: a section of highway, a single apartment building with its lights still on, a patch of farmland with rice paddies still glistening.

And then the text appeared.

**SYSTEM INITIALIZED.**

It hung in the air before his face, glowing letters of pure white light, untranslatable and yet perfectly understood. Before Ren could react, the text dissolved and reformed.

**WELCOME, SURVIVOR.**
**THE EARTH HAS BEEN INTEGRATED INTO THE INFINITE REALM.**
**YOUR SURVIVAL DEPENDS ON YOUR ABILITY TO ADAPT.**
**STATUS SCREEN UNLOCKED.**

Ren blinked. The words remained.

He thought *status*.

A panel appeared, translucent and shimmering, laid over the ruined cityscape like a heads-up display.

**NAME:** Ren Chen
**TITLE:** None
**LEVEL:** 1
**CLASS:** Unassigned
**HP:** 100/100
**QI:** 0/0

**STATS:**
STR: 5
AGI: 6
CON: 7
INT: 8
WIS: 6
CHA: 4

**ELEMENTS:**
Fire: None
Water: None
Wood: None
Metal: None
Earth: None

**AFFINITIES:**
None detected.

Ren stared at the screen. "What the hell is this? A game?"

A woman screamed behind him. He spun around, nearly tripping over a chunk of broken pavement. Fifty meters away, a man had collapsed to his knees, clutching his head. A Status Screen hovered before him too, but his was different. Red light bled from the edges.

**CLASS UNLOCKED: PYROMANCER**
**ELEMENT: FIRE — INITIALIZED**

The man—young, maybe twenty-five, wearing a hoodie and cargo pants—looked at his hands. Fire flickered between his fingers. He laughed, a sound half-terror and half-joy, and the flames grew. They licked up his arms, wrapped around his shoulders, and he didn't burn. He glowed.

"I'm a mage," he whispered. "I'm a fucking *mage*."

More Status Screens bloomed across the floating island. More people discovering what they'd become. A woman in business attire raised her hand and a spike of stone erupted from the ground. A teenager who couldn't have been older than sixteen cried out as vines burst from his skin, wrapping him in a cocoon of green.

Everyone was getting something.

Everyone but Ren.

His screen still read: **None detected.**

"What about me?" he muttered. "System, what's my element?"

The screen flickered. For a moment, nothing changed. Then the words shifted, rearranged themselves in a way that made his stomach drop.

**AFFINITIES:**
Fire: Pending
Water: Pending
Wood: Pending
Metal: Pending
Earth: Pending

**STATUS: FIVE ELEMENT AFFINITY — ALL**

Ren's breath caught.

That couldn't be right. He'd read enough web novels in his downtime—enough cultivation stories and LitRPGs—to know what this meant. Five Elements. Everyone got one. The foundation of Chinese elemental theory: Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, Water. Each one generating and overcoming the next in an eternal cycle.

No one got all five. That was impossible. That was the kind of cheat code you only saw in power fantasies.

"What the hell is this?" he said again, but this time his voice was different. Quieter. Almost afraid.

The system responded.

**FIVE ELEMENT AFFINITY: ALL**
**WARNING: SYNCHRONIZATION REQUIRED**
**WARNING: ELEMENTAL BALANCE UNSTABLE**
**WARNING: UNKNOWN CLASSIFICATION**

Unknown classification. That wasn't good.

Before Ren could process what it meant, a new notification appeared, this one pulsing with red urgency.

**ALERT: HOSTILE ENTITY DETECTED.**
**ALERT: SURVIVAL PROTOCOL ACTIVATED.**
**ALERT: YOUR FIRST ENEMY APPROACHES.**

The ground shook again. Not from tectonic shift or floating island instability. From *footsteps*.

Ren turned.

A creature was crawling over the edge of the island. It was humanoid—vaguely—but its skin was the color of rusted iron, and its limbs moved with the wrong number of joints. Its face had no eyes, only a mouth that split from ear to ear, lined with jagged teeth that looked like broken glass.

A health bar appeared above its head.

**IRON SKIN GHOUL**
**LEVEL: 3**
**HP: 450/450**

Level 3. Ren was level 1. The ghoul had four times his health. And he had no element, no class, no combat skills. He was a delivery driver with noodle arms and a bad back.

The ghoul turned its faceless head toward him. The mouth opened. Let out a sound like grinding metal.

And then it charged.

Ren's legs moved before his brain caught up. He dove behind a flipped car, and the ghoul slammed into the vehicle's side, denting the door with its shoulder. The car skidded across the pavement, and Ren scrambled to his feet, running.

"Help!" he shouted. "Someone help!"

The pyromancer was still standing in the middle of the street, admiring his flames. He looked up as Ren ran past, and for a moment, their eyes met.

Then the pyromancer smiled. "Sorry, man. I just got this. Don't want to waste it on your problem."

He turned and walked away.

Ren cursed. He kept running, but the island wasn't big. He could see the edges now, the sheer drop into the void beyond. There was nowhere to go.

The ghoul was faster. It closed the distance in seconds, and Ren felt a hand grab his shoulder, claws digging into his jacket. He was yanked off his feet, slammed onto his back, and the ghoul loomed over him, mouth opening wide.

*This is it,* Ren thought. *I'm going to die on a floating rock in some kind of apocalypse game, and I didn't even get a cool power.*

The ghoul's mouth descended.

And something inside Ren *snapped*.

It wasn't a choice. It wasn't a decision. It was a reflex, buried so deep in his bones that he didn't even know it was there until it activated. The five elements—all of them, simultaneously—surged through his body, and the world went white.

When Ren could see again, he was standing. The ghoul was ten meters away, embedded in the side of a building, its chest caved in. Steam rose from Ren's hands, and the air around him shimmered with heat.

He looked down at his arms. Earth clung to his skin like armor. Fire flickered in his palms. Water condensed into droplets that hovered around his fingers. Wood sprouted from his knuckles, tiny vines weaving between his digits. Metal—where had that come from?—formed a thin coating over his nails.

All five elements. At once.

**SYSTEM NOTIFICATION:**
**ELEMENTAL SYNCHRONIZATION COMPLETE.**
**NEW ABILITY UNLOCKED: FIVE ELEMENT FUSION.**
**NEW ENERGY TYPE DETECTED: TRUE QI.**

**QI: 50/100**

Ren stared at the numbers. Qi. Cultivators used Qi. In the stories, it was the energy that allowed martial artists to transcend human limits, to perform miracles, to break through realms of power.

The system had called this ability "Five Element Fusion." And it had created something the system didn't even have a name for.

*True Qi.*

The ghoul pulled itself out of the building. Its health bar had dropped to 320, but it was far from dead. It turned toward Ren, mouth gaping, and charged again.

Ren didn't run this time.

He didn't know what he was doing. He'd never trained, never fought, never thrown a punch that mattered. But the Qi in his body knew. It flowed through his meridians—a word he'd read a thousand times in cultivation novels but never understood until now—and it showed him what to do.

He brought his hands together. Fire and Water. Normally opposed. Under the influence of Five Element Fusion, they merged into something new. Steam exploded outward, a cloud so dense and hot that it obscured everything. The ghoul stumbled, blind.

Ren stepped to the side. Earth rose at his command, forming a spike from the ground. He grabbed it, coated it with Metal, and drove it through the ghoul's chest.

The creature shrieked. Its health bar plummeted. 200. 100. 0.

The body dissolved into motes of light, leaving behind a single glowing orb.

**VICTORY.**
**+50 XP**
**ITEM DROPPED: ELEMENTAL SHARD (UNCOMMON)**

Ren collapsed to his knees, gasping. His Qi was at 12/100. His hands were shaking. The elements receded, leaving him feeling hollow and raw.

But he was alive.

Around him, the floating island continued to drift. The pyromancer was staring, his jaw hanging open. The woman who'd summoned stone was watching too. They'd all seen it. A level 1 with no class had just killed a level 3 monster.

Ren picked up the Elemental Shard

title: The First Quest Notification
content:
The scream died in Ren's throat as he watched the building across the street fold into itself like a paper lantern consumed by invisible fire. No, not fire. Something worse. The concrete didn't burn—it simply ceased to exist, collapsing into a sphere of swirling gray nothing that hovered where the seventh floor used to be.

Ren's delivery scooter lay crushed under a chunk of asphalt the size of a refrigerator. His helmet dangled from the handlebars, one strap caught on the throttle. He'd been three meters away when the ground lurched. Three meters and a lifetime.

*Status Screen*, he thought, and the translucent interface flickered into existence before his eyes.

---

**REN WEI** | HUMAN | LEVEL 0
HP: 100/100 | MP: 50/50
STATUS: CONCUSSED (MILD)

PRIMARY AFFINITY: NONE DETECTED
HIDDEN TALENT: FIVE ELEMENT AFFINITY [LOCKED]

SURVIVAL TIMER: 23:58:12

---

The timer was counting down.

"Move," he whispered to himself. "Move your ass, Ren."

He scrambled to his feet, ignoring the sharp pain in his left ankle. Around him, Chengdu had become a nightmare symphony of screams and collapsing structures. A woman in a business suit clutched a crying child, both of them covered in dust. An elderly man knelt in the middle of the cracked street, hands pressed together in prayer. A delivery driver—another one, not Ren—lay motionless under his overturned electric tricycle.

Then the sky went dark.

Not night-dark. Wrong-dark. The kind of dark that made Ren's primitive hindbrain scream *predator*. He looked up and saw it: a crack in reality itself, a wound in the fabric of the sky that bled colors he had no name for. The crack ran from the horizon to directly overhead, and through it, he could see—*something*. Shapes that moved with impossible geometry. Mountains that floated upside down. Stars that pulsed like beating hearts.

The crack widened.

And then the notifications started.

---

**SYSTEM-WIDE ANNOUNCEMENT**
PRIME DIRECTIVE ACTIVE: EVOLVE OR PERISH
ALL SAPIENT LIFE-FORMS WILL NOW RECEIVE INDIVIDUALIZED SURVIVAL QUESTS
STAGE ONE: AWAKENING PHASE INITIATED
DURATION: 24 HOURS

---

Ren's vision swam as a second screen overlaid the first.

---

**PERSONAL QUEST: AWAKEN YOUR AFFINITY**
OBJECTIVE: LOCATE AND ABSORB ONE (1) ELEMENTAL NODE
REWARD: ELEMENTAL AFFINITY UNLOCK
PENALTY FOR FAILURE: PERMANENT STATUS AS NON-AFFINED

DIFFICULTY: F (NOVICE)
TIME LIMIT: 23:47:03

---

"Elemental node," Ren muttered, the words foreign on his tongue. "What the hell is an elemental node?"

The city screamed around him, and he knew he couldn't stay here. The crack in the sky was still spreading, and with every second, more chunks of Earth were simply *vanishing*, replaced by those floating islands he'd glimpsed in the first wave of chaos. His apartment was six blocks away. His mother's jade pendant was there. The only thing he had left of her.

He started running.

The streets were a maze of debris and panicked crowds. A man with a bloody gash across his forehead grabbed Ren's arm. "What's happening? You know what's happening?"

"No," Ren said, pulling free. "But I know we need to move."

He dodged around a burning taxi, its driver slumped over the wheel. The fire was wrong too—it burned purple, and it didn't spread. It just *was*, a contained inferno that defied physics. Ren gave it a wide berth and kept moving.

Three blocks from his apartment, he found the first body.

It was a woman in her thirties, dressed in yoga pants and a sports bra, as if she'd been heading to a morning class. She lay on her back, eyes open, staring at the fractured sky. Her chest was still, but that wasn't what made Ren stop. It was the symbol burned into her forehead: a circle with three intersecting lines, glowing faintly even in death.

---

**SYSTEM NOTIFICATION**
DECEASED LIFE-FORM DETECTED
CAUSE OF DEATH: FAILED AFFINITY AWAKENING
COLLECTING BIOMASS FOR PLANETARY RECONFIGURATION

---

The woman's body began to dissolve.

Ren watched, frozen, as her flesh unraveled into golden particles that rose like fireflies toward the crack in the sky. In thirty seconds, nothing remained but a faint outline on the asphalt, as if she'd been chalk art washed away by rain.

"Failed awakening," Ren whispered. "She tried to awaken and she *died*."

His Survival Timer ticked down: 23:42:18.

He ran faster.

His apartment building was still standing, but barely. A massive fissure ran from the foundation to the fifth floor, and the front entrance was blocked by a collapsed awning. Ren circled to the back, climbing over a pile of rubble that had once been a convenience store. The fire escape was intact, and he hauled himself up, hands bleeding from the rough metal.

Third floor. Apartment 307.

The door was unlocked. Inside, everything was exactly as he'd left it: dirty dishes in the sink, his gaming chair pushed back from the desk, a half-empty cup of cold coffee on the nightstand. The jade pendant hung from his bedpost, a small carving of a dragon wrapped around a sphere.

He grabbed it, the jade cool against his palm. His mother had given it to him when he was twelve. "For protection," she'd said, pressing it into his hands. "Your grandfather wore it in the war. Now it's yours."

He'd never believed in luck or fate or any of that. But as the pendant touched his skin, something *crackled* in the air around him. The jade grew warm, then hot, and Ren dropped it with a yelp.

The pendant hovered in midair.

"What the—"

It began to glow. Not the soft green of jade, but a brilliant white that hurt to look at. The dragon carving seemed to writhe, its tiny claws flexing, its eyes opening to reveal pupils of pure gold.

---

**SYSTEM NOTIFICATION**
ELEMENTAL ARTIFACT DETECTED
TYPE: WOOD-ASPECT PROTECTIVE TALISMAN
RANK: MORTAL (LOW)
STATUS: RESPONDING TO HOST PROXIMITY

WARNING: ARTIFACT CONTAINS TRAPPED ELEMENTAL ENERGY
RECOMMENDED ACTION: ABSORB OR RELEASE

---

Ren's hand trembled as he reached for the pendant. "Absorb," he said. "I choose absorb."

The moment his fingers touched the jade, the world went white.

Pain exploded through his chest, hot and cold at the same time, as if his veins were being filled with liquid fire and ice water simultaneously. He tried to scream, but his lungs wouldn't work. His vision flickered between the apartment and somewhere else—a forest of impossible trees with leaves of emerald fire, a river that flowed upward, a mountain that breathed.

Then, as suddenly as it began, the pain stopped.

Ren collapsed to his knees, gasping. The jade pendant lay on the floor, now pale and cracked, all its color drained. But something had changed. He could *feel* it—a new sense, like a sixth finger on his hand. The air around him seemed thicker, more alive. He could taste woodsmoke and fresh rain and green, growing things.

---

**AFFINITY AWAKENED**
PRIMARY AFFINITY: WOOD
DESCRIPTION: GROWTH, LIFE, RENEWAL
PASSIVE ABILITY: REGENERATION (MINOR)
ACTIVE ABILITY: VINE GRASP (UNLOCKED - LEVEL 1)

HIDDEN TALENT STATUS: FIVE ELEMENT AFFINITY [1/5 UNLOCKED]

---

Ren stared at the screen. "One out of five," he breathed. "I need all five elements to unlock the hidden talent."

The apartment shook, and through the window, he saw more buildings collapsing. More golden particles rising. The crack in the sky had grown wider, and through it, he could now see entire continents floating in the void beyond. The islands he'd glimpsed earlier were coming closer, descending through the crack like falling stars.

And with them came *things*.

Creatures that had no business existing in any sane reality. Some were insectoid, with too many legs and eyes that burned like embers. Others were humanoid but wrong—too tall, too thin, their limbs bending at angles that shouldn't be possible. They dropped from the floating islands, landing on the shattered streets of Chengdu with predatory grace.

A new notification appeared, pulsing red.

---

**STAGE ONE UPDATE**
HOSTILE BIOMORPHS DETECTED
CLASSIFICATION: ELEMENTAL BEASTS (LOW GRADE)
THREAT LEVEL: VARIABLE

NEW OBJECTIVE ADDED TO PERSONAL QUEST:
SURVIVE THE AWAKENING PHASE
BONUS REWARD FOR KILLING 5+ ELEMENTAL BEASTS:
RANDOM ELEMENTAL ABILITY UPGRADE

---

Ren grabbed the cracked jade pendant and shoved it in his pocket. He didn't know how to fight. He was a delivery driver, not a soldier. But the Survival Timer was still ticking, and now he knew exactly what happened to people who failed to evolve.

The woman in yoga pants. Dissolved into particles. Gone.

*Not me*, Ren thought. *I'm not dying today.*

He moved to the window, peering down at the street. Three of the biomorphs were prowling below—insectoid things with scorpion tails and too many legs. One of them stopped, its head swiveling toward the apartment building. Toward *him*.

It knew he was there.

Ren backed away from the window, his heart hammering. The Wood affinity thrummed in his chest, and he focused on it, trying to understand what it could do. *Vine Grasp*, the notification had said. *Active ability.*

He held out his hand, palm open, and *willed*.

Nothing happened at first. Then he felt it—a stirring in the floorboards beneath his feet, a whisper of growth in the wooden frame of the building. A crack appeared in the floor, and from it, a green shoot emerged, growing at impossible speed. It twisted and thickened, forming a rope of living wood that coiled around Ren's arm.

"Okay," he said, his voice shaking. "Okay, I can work with this."

The biomorph below screeched, a sound like grinding metal. More of them answered, a chorus of inhuman calls that echoed through the ruined city. Ren looked out the window again and saw them converging, drawn by some signal he couldn't perceive.

Dozens of them. Maybe hundreds.

His Survival Timer read 23:31:44.

He had less than a day to find the other four elemental nodes. Less than a day to unlock the hidden talent that the system had marked as "LOCKED." Less than a day before the biomorphs found him and tore him apart.

The jade pendant pulsed against his leg, and for just a moment, he felt a

title: The First Night of Butchery
content:
The sky bled orange and purple as the last sunlight died behind the fractured horizon.

Ren stood on what remained of Wuhou Temple's eastern plaza, his hands still trembling from the morning's chaos. Eight hours had passed since the integration. Eight hours since humanity learned they were livestock in a cosmic game.

And in those eight hours, the population of Chengdu's surviving fragments had dropped by forty percent.

"We can't stay here." The voice came from behind him—a woman named Lin Wei, former tour guide at the temple, now the unofficial leader of their ragtag group of thirty-seven survivors. Her status screen marked her as [Water Affinity: Tier 1]. "The system said we have seven days before the next phase. Whatever that means, we need walls. Defenses."

Ren didn't turn around. He was staring at his own status screen, still unable to believe what it showed.

---

**REN ZHAN**
**Talent: Five Element Affinity - ALL**
**Cultivation: None (Potential: Infinite)**
**Active Elements: Metal (Tier 0), Wood (Tier 0), Water (Tier 0), Fire (Tier 0), Earth (Tier 0)**
**Qi: 0/100 (Locked)**
**Status: Unawakened**

---

*Unawakened.*

The word mocked him. He had all five elements—something the system's own documentation called "theoretical impossibility"—but he couldn't use any of them. Not yet. The system demanded he *awaken* his affinity through some method it refused to explain.

Meanwhile, the other survivors had already started leveling. Lin Wei could summon water from nothing, enough to fill three canteens an hour. A retired soldier named Chen Feng had [Metal Affinity: Tier 1] and could harden his skin to steel for thirty seconds at a time. Even a sixteen-year-old girl named Mei had awakened [Fire Affinity: Tier 1]—though she'd accidentally burned down a souvenir shop in the process.

And Ren? Ren had nothing but a status screen that glowed with impossible colors and a countdown timer he couldn't stop checking.

**Days Until Phase 2: 6**

"Ren. Are you listening?"

He blinked. Lin Wei had moved closer, her face tight with worry. She was in her late thirties, with sharp eyes that missed nothing. "I said we need to move. There's a monster wave coming."

"What?"

She pointed east, toward what used to be the Jin River. The water was gone now—just a chasm of darkness separating their island from the next fragment. But something was crawling up from that darkness.

*Monster Wave: 5 minutes*

The system notification appeared in the air above them, visible to everyone. A ripple of fear went through the group.

"Everyone to the temple entrance!" Lin Wei shouted. "Chen Feng, you're on the front line. Mei, conserve your fire—only use it when they're inside the gate. The rest of you, grab anything that can be used as a weapon."

Ren moved with the others, his heart hammering. He grabbed a broken wooden beam from the rubble—pathetic, but better than nothing. Around him, he saw the fear in their faces. These were office workers, students, retirees. None of them had ever fought for their lives before today.

*Monster Wave: 2 minutes*

The first creatures appeared at the edge of the fragment.

They looked like dogs—if dogs were made of cracked stone and glowing red crystals. Each one was the size of a German shepherd, with jaws that split into four distinct mandibles. Their eyes burned like embers.

**[Stone Maw Hound - Tier 1]**
**HP: 80/80**

"Hold the line!" Chen Feng roared, his skin taking on a metallic sheen. He stood at the temple's broken gate, a steel rebar in his hands.

The hounds charged.

The first one leaped at Chen Feng. He swung the rebar in a brutal arc, catching it in the side of the head. The impact sounded like rock hitting rock. The hound tumbled, but immediately scrambled back up, its HP bar showing only minor damage.

*62/80*

"They're tough!" Chen Feng grunted.

Then the rest of the pack hit.

It was chaos. The survivors screamed, swung, died. A middle-aged man named Zhang tried to block a hound's charge with a trash can lid. The creature's mandibles punched through the metal and into his chest. He was dead before he hit the ground.

Lin Wei summoned water, shaping it into a spear that she drove through a hound's eye. It howled, thrashing, but didn't die. Mei sent a burst of fire across the pack's flank, forcing them to scatter. Chen Feng held the gate, but he was bleeding from a dozen cuts, his metal skin flickering as his energy ran low.

And Ren stood in the middle of it all, useless.

His wooden beam shattered against the first hound that came for him. He dodged left, felt the wind of its jaws passing inches from his throat. This was it. This was how he died—with a system that promised infinite power but delivered nothing.

*Why?* he screamed internally. *Why give me everything if I can't use any of it?*

**Soul Resonance Detected.**

The system notification appeared in front of him, shimmering with light that only he could see.

**Your emotional frequency matches the requirements for [Five Element Affinity - ALL] awakening.**

**Warning: Unstable awakening may result in permanent soul damage.**

**Proceed?**

A hound lunged at him, jaws wide.

Ren didn't have time to think. He slammed his palm against the notification.

**YES.**

The world exploded into color.

He felt it all at once—the metal in the ground beneath his feet, the wood in the temple pillars behind him, the water vapor in the air, the fire in the hound's burning eyes, the earth of the fragment itself. Five distinct energies, each singing a different song, each demanding his attention.

And then he felt the space between them. The void where they *should* have combined. The missing piece.

*Qi.*

The system had said his potential was infinite. But potential meant nothing without cultivation. Without the method to transform these five raw elements into something greater.

But the system had also said something else. Something he'd barely registered in the morning's chaos.

*Note: True Qi can be generated through [Five Element Resonance]—a state achieved when all five elements are simultaneously awakened in a host body.*

Simultaneously awakened.

He was already awakened. He just hadn't *accessed* it.

Ren reached inside himself, not with his hands but with his awareness. He found the five points of light in his core—dim, dormant, but present. Metal in his bones. Wood in his sinews. Water in his blood. Fire in his breath. Earth in his flesh.

And he pulled.

The hound's jaws closed on his arm—

—and stopped.

Not because of any shield or barrier. Because Ren's arm had become something else. Something the hound's stone teeth couldn't penetrate.

**[Five Element Infusion: Earth - Tier 0]**
**Effect: Temporary conversion of flesh to stone. Duration: 3 seconds.**

Three seconds. That was all he got.

But three seconds was enough.

Ren grabbed the hound's upper jaw with his stone-hardened hand, and with the other hand—still flesh—he *pulled*.

**[Five Element Infusion: Metal - Tier 0]**
**Effect: Temporary reinforcement of grip strength.**

The hound's jaw shattered in his grip.

It yelped, trying to retreat, but Ren was already moving. He didn't know what he was doing. His body moved on instinct, the five elements flowing through him in a chaotic dance. He felt the water in the hound's blood, the earth in its bones, the metal in its crystals.

And he *twisted*.

The hound's body crumpled, its own elements turning against it. It died with a whimper, its body collapsing into a pile of dust and crystal fragments.

**[Kill Confirmed: Stone Maw Hound - Tier 1]**
**Experience Gained: 50 XP**
**Elemental Proficiency: Earth +1, Metal +1**

The system notifications kept coming as Ren turned to face the rest of the pack.

There were twelve left.

He had three seconds of stone skin, a grip that could shatter jaws, and a body that was screaming from the strain of channeling five different energies at once.

And he had never felt more alive.

"Ren!" Lin Wei's voice cut through the chaos. "Your arm—it's bleeding—but it's also *glowing*—"

He looked down. His right arm was indeed bleeding from a dozen cuts, but beneath the blood, his skin pulsed with a soft golden light. The same light that had appeared when he activated his first element.

*Five Element Resonance.*

The system had called it a theoretical state. But theory was becoming reality.

Ren launched himself at the nearest hound, not waiting to understand what he was doing. His body knew. His soul knew. All he had to do was *let go*.

The second hound died faster than the first. A simple touch to its flank, and he felt the water in its body boil from the inside. [Water Element] and [Fire Element] combined in a burst of steam that erupted from the creature's skin.

The third hound he met head-on, hardening his fist with [Earth] and sharpening it with [Metal]. His punch went through its skull like a hammer through clay.

The fourth, fifth, and sixth fell in rapid succession. Each kill taught him something new. Each element combination revealed a different technique. Water and Wood created a vine-like whip that could constrict and drain. Fire and Metal let him superheat the air around his fists. Earth and Water turned the ground beneath the hounds' feet into quicksand.

By the time the last hound fell, Ren was covered in blood—his own and theirs—and the golden light had spread across his entire body.

**[Combat Complete]**
**17 Kills**
**Experience Gained: 850 XP**
**Elemental Proficiency: All Elements +2**
**Title Unlocked: [First Awakening]**

The survivors stared at him in silence.

Lin Wei's mouth hung open. Chen Feng had dropped his rebar. Mei's fire had gone out, her eyes wide as she looked at Ren like he was something other than human.

"Ren," Lin Wei finally managed. "What... what are you?"

He opened his mouth to answer—

**SYSTEM ALERT**

—and the world went dark.

Not the darkness of night. The darkness of *absence*. As if someone had turned off the universe itself.

**Congratulations, host Ren Zhan.**

**You have successfully achieved [Five Element Awakening].**

**You are the first human to accomplish this in the current iteration.**

**Iterations: 1,247,891**

**Previous successful hosts: 0**

**Probability of success: 0.0000000000000001%**

**You are not supposed to exist.**

**Administrator attention has been flagged.**

**Estimated response time: 71 hours, 48 minutes, 12 seconds.**

**Recommendation: Reach [True Qi] before Administrator arrival.**

**Failure condition: Permanent deletion from all iterations.**

The darkness lifted as suddenly as it had come.

Ren was still standing in the temple plaza. The survivors were still staring at him. The hounds' corpses were still scattered across the broken stone.

But everything felt different.

The air itself seemed *heavier*, as if something vast and terrible had turned its attention toward this tiny fragment of what used to be Earth.

Se

title: Chapter 4
content:
CHAPTER 4: The Weight of Attention

Ren's legs gave out.

He didn't fall gracefully. His knees buckled, his spine twisted, and he landed hard on his side, sending a spike of pain through his ribs. The golden light flickered and died, leaving him pale and trembling on the blood-slicked stone.

"Ren!" Lin Wei was at his side in seconds, her hands pressing against his shoulders. "What happened? Your eyes—they went completely white—"

"I saw something." The words came out as a croak. "Something that... shouldn't exist."

The system message still burned in his mind. *1,247,891 iterations. Zero previous successes. Administrator attention flagged.*

*What the hell is an Administrator?*

Chen Feng approached cautiously, dragging his rebar behind him like a nervous tail. "Is he dying? He looks like he's dying."

"Shut up and help me get him upright." Lin Wei hooked her arms under Ren's shoulders. "Mei, check the perimeter. Those hounds might have friends."

Mei nodded and jogged toward the edge of the plaza, her fire elemental flickering back to life in her palm. The other survivors—maybe thirty in total—huddled together near the broken statue of what had once been a minor Chinese deity. They watched Ren with a mixture of fear and desperate hope.

*They saw what I did,* Ren realized. *They saw me kill seventeen creatures with my bare hands. They're scared of me.*

Good. Let them be scared. Fear kept people alive.

Lin Wei and Chen Feng dragged him to the base of the statue and propped him against its cracked pedestal. Ren's body felt like it had been put through a meat grinder. Every muscle screamed. Every joint ached. His elemental channels—whatever those were—felt like they'd been stretched past their breaking point.

**[Status: Severe Elemental Fatigue]**
**[Recovery Time: 4-6 hours without intervention]**
**[Warning: Repeated overuse may cause permanent channel damage]**

"Drink this." Lin Wei pressed a canteen to his lips. Water—real water, not elemental—flooded his parched throat. He drank until his stomach protested, then drank more.

"What was that light?" Chen Feng asked. He'd dropped the tough-guy act completely. His voice had a tremor in it. "When you killed those things... you were glowing like a damn angel."

"I don't know." Ren wiped his mouth with the back of his hand. "The system called it Five Element Awakening. Said I'm the first person to achieve it."

"First person?" Lin Wei's brow furrowed. "Out of everyone on Earth?"

"Out of... everyone. Ever." Ren didn't elaborate. He couldn't. How did you explain 1.2 million failed timelines to someone who was still processing the fact that gravity had stopped working twelve hours ago?

Mei returned at a jog, her face tight. "No more hounds. But we have another problem."

"Of course we do." Ren tried to stand, failed, and settled for sitting upright. "What now?"

"The east side of the island." Mei pointed toward a cluster of crumbling buildings that had once been part of a university campus. "There's a group of people. Maybe fifty. They've got a leader."

"And?"

"And he's claiming to be a government official. Says he's establishing a 'Provisional Administrative Zone.'" Mei's lip curled. "He's already executed three people for 'hoarding resources.'"

Chen Feng snorted. "Government? What government? The country's in a thousand pieces."

"Tell that to his enforcers." Mei's fire elemental flickered higher. "They're all awakened. Maybe a dozen of them. Different elements. And they're armed."

Ren forced himself to stand. His legs screamed, but they held. "Show me."

"Ren, you can barely walk—" Lin Wei started.

"I can still fight." He looked at her. "And I need to see what we're dealing with. If there's someone trying to set up a dictatorship in the first twelve hours of the apocalypse, we need to know now. Before they get too strong to stop."

Lin Wei held his gaze for a long moment, then nodded. "Mei, lead the way. Chen, stay here with the civilians. Keep them quiet and keep them alive."

"What am I supposed to do if those hounds come back?"

"Run." Lin Wei grabbed a length of rebar from the ground. "Or don't. Your choice."

They moved through the ruined streets in silence. The island float—maybe two square kilometers in total—had been part of Chengdu's eastern district. Ren recognized bits and pieces: a noodle shop he'd delivered to a hundred times, now split in half by the dimensional fracture; an apartment building whose upper floors had been sheared off completely, exposing bedrooms and living rooms like a dollhouse; a bus crushed flat by debris.

The sky was wrong. That was the thing that kept hitting Ren. The sky was *wrong*. There were no clouds, no stars, no moon. Just an endless gray-black void that seemed to press down on everything. And in the distance, other islands floated like broken teeth, some close enough to jump to, others barely visible as specks of light.

*How do we get off this rock?* Ren thought. *And where would we even go?*

The system didn't answer. It never answered the important questions.

Mei stopped at the corner of a collapsed convenience store and pointed. "There. The main square."

Ren peered around the edge. The square had once been a public gathering space with a fountain at its center. Now the fountain was dry, and the space was occupied by a makeshift camp. Tents—salvaged from who knows where—formed a rough circle around a central bonfire. People moved between them, their movements purposeful and efficient.

And at the center of it all stood a man.

He was tall, broad-shouldered, with a military bearing that spoke of years of training. He wore a dark blue uniform that might have been police or might have been military—it was hard to tell in the flickering firelight. A pistol hung at his hip, and his eyes scanned the crowd with the cold calculation of a predator.

"He calls himself Captain Zhao," Mei whispered. "Says he was with the People's Armed Police. Claims he's been sent by the 'Central Emergency Response Bureau.'"

"That's not a real thing," Lin Wei said.

"Doesn't matter if it's real. People believe him." Mei pointed at the crowd. "See how they're organized? He's got them sorted by skills. Construction types on the north end. Medical personnel near the fire. Combat-capable on the perimeter. He's been at this for hours."

Ren studied the camp. Mei was right—the organization was impressive. Far better than the chaos they had back at the temple. Captain Zhao had turned a disaster into a functioning settlement in less than a day.

*Which means he's either a genuine leader... or a very dangerous tyrant.*

"I need to talk to him."

"Ren—" Lin Wei grabbed his arm. "You can barely stand. And he's got a dozen awakened enforcers. If he decides you're a threat—"

"Then I need to know that now." Ren gently pulled his arm free. "Before he decides to 'incorporate' our group by force. Stay here. If I don't come back in fifteen minutes, take the survivors and find another island. Jump if you have to."

"We don't even know if jumping works!"

"Then figure it out." Ren stepped out from behind the building and walked toward the camp.

He didn't try to hide. He didn't try to look threatening. He just walked, hands visible, pace steady, straight toward the bonfire.

The enforcers saw him immediately. A woman with earth-elemental armor—stone plates covering her arms and chest—stepped into his path. "Stop. This is a controlled zone. State your business."

"I'm here to talk to Captain Zhao." Ren kept his voice calm. "I represent a group of survivors at the temple plaza. I want to discuss cooperation."

The woman's eyes narrowed. "You're the one who killed those hounds. We saw the light show from here."

"Guilty."

"Impressive." She didn't sound impressed. She sounded wary. "Wait here."

She disappeared into the camp. Ren stood in the darkness, feeling the weight of a dozen eyes on him. The enforcers had spread out in a loose semicircle, blocking his escape routes. They were professionals. Whoever this Captain Zhao was, he'd trained his people well.

A minute passed. Two. Then the woman returned. "Captain will see you. Leave your weapons here."

"I don't have any weapons."

She looked at his blood-caked hands. "Right. Follow me."

The camp parted around them as they walked. People stared at Ren with a mixture of curiosity and fear. He heard whispers—"the glowing man," "elemental killer," "maybe he's the one"—and tried not to let them get to his head.

Captain Zhao stood by the bonfire, a cup of tea in his hand. Up close, he was older than Ren had expected—maybe forty-five, with graying temples and deep lines around his eyes. But his posture was ramrod straight, and his eyes missed nothing.

"So." Zhao's voice was deep, resonant. "You're the awakened who single-handedly killed seventeen void hounds. Impressive, for a first-day civilian."

"I had help." Ren stopped a few meters away. "But yes."

"Your element?"

"All of them."

A flicker of surprise crossed Zhao's face before he suppressed it. "All of them? That's not possible. The system assigns one element per person. Sometimes two, for the lucky ones."

"The system assigned me five." Ren met his gaze. "I don't know why. I don't know how. But I have Metal, Wood, Water, Fire, and Earth."

Zhao was silent for a long moment. Then he laughed—a short, sharp sound. "You're either the luckiest man alive or the most dangerous. I haven't decided which."

"Can I ask you a question?"

"You can ask."

"Are you actually with the government?"

Zhao's smile faded. "Does it matter?"

"To me? Yes."

"Then no." Zhao set down his tea. "I was a security guard at a bank. Never served a day in the military. But people need order, and I can provide it. The title helps them feel safe."

Ren felt a grudging respect for the man's honesty. "And the executions?"

"Three men who tried to hoard food and weapons while others starved. I gave them a trial. They confessed. The penalty was death." Zhao's eyes hardened. "I don't enjoy it. But in a world without laws, someone has to make them. And someone has to enforce them."

"Is that what you want from me? Enforcement?"

"No." Zhao stepped closer. "I want to know what you are. The system flagged something when you awakened. I felt it. A shift in the world's pressure, like a giant stepping closer." He lowered his voice. "And I received a message. A system message. It said: 'Administrator interest detected. Prepare for contact protocol.' Do you know what that means?"

Ren's blood ran cold. "You saw that too?"

"Everyone in a kilometer radius saw it. The system broadcast it." Zhao's face was grim. "Whatever you've become, Ren Zhan—the whole world knows you exist now. And something is coming to investigate."

The fire crackled. The void sky pressed down. And somewhere in the darkness between worlds, a clock was ticking.

71 hours, 11 minutes remaining.

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER 5: The Weight of Attention

The golden light faded from Ren's skin, but the *pressure* remained.

It pressed down on his shoulders like a physical weight, made his bones ache and his teeth grind together. The air had become thick, viscous. Each breath required conscious effort.

"Ren?" Lin Wei stepped toward him, her hand outstretched. "Are you—"

"Stay back."

The words came out harsher than he intended. Lin Wei froze, hurt flickering across her face before she masked it.

Chen Feng's rebar clattered as he picked it up. "What the hell happened? One second you're glowing like a Christmas tree, next second the whole world went dark. And now you're—" He gestured vaguely at Ren. "—whatever this is."

Ren didn't answer. He was too busy reading the notification that still hovered at the edge of his vision.

**Administrator attention has been flagged.**
**Estimated response time: 71 hours, 48 minutes, 12 seconds.**
**Recommendation: Reach [True Qi] before Administrator arrival.**

"Three days," he muttered.

"What?" Mei stepped forward, her fire flickering back to life in her palm. "What's three days?"

Ren looked up at the survivors. Twenty-three people. A handful of wounded. No supplies. No shelter. No way off this floating rock.

And something was coming to delete him from existence.

"The system," he said slowly, "didn't expect me to survive. I'm... a glitch. And glitches get patched."

The temple plaza fell silent. Somewhere in the distance, a piece of shattered stone broke free from the island's edge and tumbled into the void.

"So what do we do?" Lin Wei's voice was steady. Professional. She had shifted back into paramedic mode, compartmentalizing the fear. "You said we need to reach True Qi. How?"

Ren shook his head. "I don't know. The system mentioned it as a theoretical state. Something beyond elemental manipulation. But I've been operating on instinct since this started."

"Then keep operating on instinct." Chen Feng stepped forward, his jaw set. "You just killed seventeen of those hellhounds without knowing what you were doing. Imagine what you could do if you actually *tried*."

"That's not—"

"He's right." Mei's fire flared brighter. "You're the only one here who can fight. The rest of us have one element each. You have five. And you combined them. I saw it. Water and Fire. Earth and Metal. You created *new* elements, Ren. Do you understand how insane that is?"

Ren opened his mouth to argue, then stopped.

She was right. He had combined elements instinctively. Created hybrid techniques that shouldn't exist according to the basic tutorials the system had provided. Water and Fire shouldn't work together. They were opposites. But when he had touched that second hound, he had felt the water molecules inside its body vibrate, felt the heat build until they turned to steam, felt the creature's flesh tear apart from the inside.

*Opposites don't cancel each other*, he realized. *They complete each other.*

"Mei," he said abruptly, "show me your fire."

She blinked. "What?"

"Your fire. I need to see it up close."

Mei exchanged a glance with Lin Wei, then shrugged. She extended her palm, and the flame grew, casting dancing shadows across the temple walls. It was a simple orange-red fire, the kind you'd see in a campfire or a hearth. Pure. Uncomplicated.

Ren reached out his hand.

"Are you insane?" Lin Wei grabbed his arm. "That's fire. Real fire. It'll burn you."

"I know."

"Then don't—"

"I need to understand it." Ren gently pulled his arm free. "The system said I have Five Element Affinity. But I don't know what that means. I've been reacting, not acting. If I'm going to reach True Qi in three days, I need to *know* the elements, not just use them."

He pressed his palm into Mei's flame.

The pain was immediate and excruciating. His skin blistered, the flesh beneath sizzling. But beneath the pain, he felt something else. A pattern. A rhythm. The fire wasn't chaos—it was a dance of molecules, a release of stored energy, a constant hunger for fuel.

And he could feel it. Not just the heat, but the *structure*.

**[Fire Element Proficiency +1]**
**[New Insight: Fire is consumption. Fire is transformation. Fire is the bridge between what was and what will be.]**

Ren pulled his hand back. The burns were severe, third-degree at least. But as he watched, the golden light beneath his skin pulsed, and the flesh began to knit itself back together.

"Holy shit," Chen Feng breathed.

"Wood," Ren said, staring at his healing hand. "Wood represents growth, life, renewal. When combined with Water, it accelerates natural processes. But I'm not using Wood. I'm using something else."

"Your body's healing itself," Lin Wei said, her medical training overriding her shock. She grabbed his wrist, examining the rapidly closing wound. "That's not possible. Third-degree burns don't heal in seconds."

"It's not healing," Ren corrected. "It's reversing. The system says True Qi is beyond the elements. What if it's not a new power, but a return to something older? Something that existed before the elements were separated?"

The survivors looked at him blankly.

"Forget it." Ren stood up, flexing his now-perfect hand. "We need to move. This island is too exposed. The system knows I'm here. If it sent those hounds as a test, it'll send something worse as a response."

"Where do we go?" Mei asked.

Ren looked around the temple plaza. The building itself was mostly intact, but it sat at the highest point of the island. Exposed on all sides. A death trap if anything with ranged attacks showed up.

"There." He pointed toward the island's edge, where a forest of twisted trees clung to the cliffside. "Cover. Water source. We can set up a perimeter."

"And if something attacks us in the forest?" Chen Feng asked.

"Then we'll have trees to burn and shadows to hide in." Ren started walking. "Stay close. Stay quiet. And if I tell you to run, don't argue."

---

The forest was darker than Ren expected.

The trees were alien, their bark black and slick, their leaves a deep purple that seemed to absorb light rather than reflect it. The ground was soft, almost spongy, and every step released a faint chemical smell.

"This place is wrong," Lin Wei whispered. She had fallen into step beside Ren, her medical kit bouncing against her hip. "I've never seen trees like this."

"They're adapting." Ren touched one of the trunks. The bark was warm, almost feverish. "The system said Earth shattered into 10,000 islands. Each one is a new ecosystem. New rules. New dangers."

"New ways to die."

"New ways to survive."

They pushed deeper into the forest. The canopy grew thicker, blocking out the artificial light that passed for day in this broken world. Ren's senses were on high alert, the elemental energy inside him humming like a live wire.

He could feel everything.

The water in the soil. The metal deposits beneath the surface. The wood of the trees, alive and growing. The fire that slept at the island's core, waiting for a spark. The earth itself, fragile and fractured, held together by nothing but the system's whim.

And something else.

Something that *didn't belong*.

"Stop." Ren held up his fist. The group halted behind him.

"What is it?" Mei's fire flickered to life, casting nervous shadows.

"There's something ahead. Not a hound. Bigger." Ren closed his eyes, reaching out with his elemental senses. "It's... made of Metal. And Earth. And something I don't recognize."

"A golem?" Chen Feng suggested.

"Maybe. Or a construct." Ren opened his eyes. "The system's administrator flagged my location. They might have sent a cleanup crew."

"So what do we do?" Lin Wei asked.

Ren looked at the survivors. Twenty-three people. Exhausted. Terrified. Looking to him for answers.

"We go around," he said finally. "We find shelter. We survive tonight. Tomorrow, we figure out how to reach True Qi."

"And if we can't?"

Ren didn't answer. He couldn't.

They moved again, circling wide around the metallic presence Ren had sensed. The forest grew denser, the trees pressing closer together until they were forced to walk single file. The chemical smell grew stronger, and Ren began to feel lightheaded.

"Something's wrong," he muttered.

"Besides everything?" Chen Feng's voice was strained.

"The air. It's different. There's something in it."

As if on cue, a woman at the back of the group collapsed. Then another. Then a third.

"Gas!" Lin Wei dropped to her knees, checking the nearest victim. "They're not breathing—their lungs are filling with fluid—"

"Water," Ren said, understanding dawning. "The trees are releasing water vapor. But it's not normal water. It's... altered. Poisoned."

**[Alert: Toxic Elemental Exposure Detected]**
**[Source: Altered Water Element]**
**[Recommendation: Purge contaminants immediately or evacuate area]**

"Everyone out!" Ren shouted. "Back the way we came!"

But as the survivors turned to flee, the trees *moved*.

Branches that had hung limp and lifeless suddenly whipped through the air, catching two people before they could scream. The branches wrapped around their limbs, their throats, and dragged them into the darkness of the forest.

"Don't stop!" Ren grabbed Lin Wei's arm, pulling her toward the exit. "Run!"

They ran.

The forest came alive around them. Roots erupted from the ground, tripping and grabbing. Branches swung down like clubs. The purple leaves released clouds of toxic vapor that burned the lungs and blurred the vision.

Ren fought back. Fire erupted from his palms, scorching the branches that reached for him. Earth rose in walls, blocking the roots. Metal sharpened the air itself, slicing through the attacking vegetation.

But for every branch he burned, three more took its place. For every root he crushed, a dozen more emerged.

The survivors were falling. One by one. Dragged into the darkness.

By the time they burst free of the forest, only twelve remained.

Ren turned to face the tree line, his chest heaving, his body screaming. The forest had gone still. Silent. As if it had never attacked at all.

"What the hell was that?" Chen Feng gasped, leaning on his rebar.

Ren shook his head. He was looking at the survivors. Twelve. From twenty-three. In less than five minutes.

"We need to rest," Lin Wei said. "Those who inhaled the gas need treatment."

"Not here." Ren scanned the area. They had emerged on a rocky outcropping near the island's edge. Below them, the void stretched endlessly. "We need higher ground."

"There's a cave." A young man Ren didn't recognize pointed to a dark opening in the cliff face. "I saw it during the panic."

Ren looked at the cave. It was small, barely large enough for two people to enter side by side. Defensible. But a potential death trap if they got trapped inside.

"We check it," he said finally. "Mei, light the way. Chen Feng, guard the rear. Everyone else, stay between us."

They approached the cave cautiously. The opening was natural, worn smooth by wind and water. Inside, it opened into a chamber large enough for

title: The Administrator's Shadow
content:
The golden light faded from Ren's skin, but the weight in the air only grew heavier. He could feel it pressing against his bones, a pressure that had nothing to do with gravity and everything to do with *intent*. Something was watching. Something old. Something that had noticed him.

"Ren." Lin Wei stepped forward, her crossbow still smoking. "Your eyes. They're... different."

He raised a hand to his face. His fingers came away slick with blood, but beneath the crimson, he could see it—a faint golden sheen coating his pupils like liquid metal. The world looked sharper through that sheen. He could see the elemental composition of everything around him. The iron in Lin Wei's crossbow. The carbon in Chen Feng's rebar. The water vapor suspended in the air between them.

*Five Element Awakening.*

The system's message echoed in his skull. *1,247,891 iterations. Zero previous successes. Administrator attention flagged.*

"What does that mean?" he muttered. "What's an Administrator?"

Mei's fire flared back to life, casting flickering shadows across the plaza. "You're talking to yourself. That's usually not a good sign after someone turns into a human light bulb."

"He's not talking to himself." Chen Feng's voice was flat, clinical. He'd picked up his rebar again, but he wasn't pointing it at Ren. He was pointing it at the sky. "He's talking to *that*."

Everyone followed his gaze.

The sky above the floating island had changed. Where there had been darkness and distant stars, there was now a crack. A thin, jagged line of pure white light, like a scar torn across the fabric of reality. It pulsed with a rhythm that matched Ren's heartbeat.

*One.*

The crack widened.

*Two.*

Ren felt something *pull* at his chest, as if a hook had been lodged in his soul.

*Three.*

"That's not supposed to be there." Lin Wei's voice was barely a whisper. "The system said the islands were sealed. No outside interference for thirty days."

"Seventy-one hours." Ren's throat was dry. "That's how long I have before they get here."

"Before *who* gets here?" Mei demanded.

"The Administrators." The word tasted like ash in his mouth. "The system called them that. It said I'm not supposed to exist. That my awakening flagged their attention."

Chen Feng's jaw tightened. "Attention means interest. Interest means investigation. And investigation, on a world that's been shattered into ten thousand pieces, means nothing good."

"He's right." A new voice cut through the tension, rough and tired. An old man emerged from the shadows of the temple's collapsed entrance. He leaned on a walking stick carved from black wood, and his eyes were the pale gray of storm clouds. "The Administrators don't investigate. They *prune*."

Lin Wei's crossbow swung toward him. "Who the hell are you?"

"Someone who's been here longer than you." The old man shuffled closer, his stick clicking against the broken stone. "Name's Zhang Wei. I was a professor of theoretical physics at Tsinghua University. Before the Shattering, that is. Now I'm just an old man who's seen too much."

Ren studied him. The man's elemental composition was strange—a mix of all five elements, but twisted, corrupted, like a chord played out of tune. "You're an Awakened."

"Was." Zhang Wei stopped a few meters away. "I was a Five Element user too. Once."

The words hit Ren like a physical blow. "You—"

"Failed." The old man's laugh was bitter. "I failed the same awakening you just achieved. I got to the edge of Five Element Resonance, but I couldn't take the final step. The Administrators noticed me anyway. They sent a *Regulator*."

"What happened?" Mei asked.

Zhang Wei pulled up his sleeve. His arm was a patchwork of scar tissue, the skin twisted and fused like melted wax. But beneath the scars, Ren could see it—a faint black corrosion that pulsed with the same rhythm as the crack in the sky.

"The Regulator didn't kill me. That would have been too clean." The old man's voice dropped to a whisper. "It *marked* me. Left a piece of itself inside my soul. Now I'm a beacon. A tracking device. Every time someone on this island awakens a new element, the Regulator feels it. And it comes closer."

"How long have you been hiding it?" Chen Feng's grip on his rebar tightened.

"Since day one." Zhang Wei met his gaze without flinching. "I've been watching you all. Waiting to see if anyone would succeed where I failed. And now that you have..." He turned to Ren. "The Regulator will be here within the hour."

"An hour?" Lin Wei's voice cracked. "We can't fight something that—"

"We can't fight it at all." Zhang Wei cut her off. "Not yet. Not without True Qi."

The system had mentioned that. *Reach True Qi before Administrator arrival.* Ren's fists clenched. "What is True Qi? How do I reach it?"

"Qi is the fusion of all five elements into a single energy source. True Qi is the *perfection* of that fusion." Zhang Wei's eyes glowed with a faint internal light. "I've spent the past week studying. The old temple has records. Carvings. Diagrams. The first generation of Awakened on this island—the ones who died in the initial wave—they left instructions."

"Show me."

The old man nodded and turned toward the temple. Ren followed, the others falling into step behind him. The crack in the sky pulsed with each step he took, a constant reminder of the clock ticking down.

The temple's interior was worse than the exterior. The ceiling had collapsed in several places, and the walls were covered in cracks that spiderwebbed toward the ceiling. But the floor was different. The floor was covered in carvings—intricate patterns of circles and lines and characters that glowed with a faint golden light.

"The Five Element Wheel." Zhang Wei pointed at the largest carving, a circle divided into five sections, each representing a different element. Metal, Wood, Water, Fire, Earth. "This is the foundation. Each element flows into the next, creating a closed loop. But True Qi requires something more."

He traced his finger along the center of the circle. "It requires a *core*. A point of convergence where all five elements meet and *annihilate*."

"Annihilate?" Lin Wei's brow furrowed. "That sounds like destruction, not creation."

"Destruction and creation are the same thing." Zhang Wei's voice took on a lecturing tone. "The Big Bang was destruction. The death of a star is creation. To create True Qi, you must destroy the boundaries between the elements. You must force them to collide with such force that they cease to exist as separate entities and become something new."

Ren stared at the carving. The pattern was beautiful, complex, *alive*. He could feel the elements within it, pulsing in a rhythm that matched his own heartbeat.

"How do I do it?"

"You don't." Zhang Wei's voice was flat. "Not yet. The first step is to stabilize your current awakening. Right now, your elements are raw, uncontrolled. Every time you use them, you risk burning out your channels."

"What does that mean?"

"It means your body isn't ready for the power you've unlocked." The old man reached into his robe and pulled out a small jade disk, carved with the same pattern as the floor. "This is a regulator. A tool the original Awakened used to control their element flow. It'll buy you time."

Ren took the disk. It was cold, smooth, and hummed with a faint energy that resonated with his own. "How does it work?"

"Press it against your chest and focus on balancing your elements. The disk will do the rest." Zhang Wei stepped back. "But be careful. The disk is a crutch, not a cure. Rely on it too long, and your body will forget how to regulate itself."

Ren nodded and pressed the disk to his chest. The moment it touched his skin, he felt a shift—a settling of the chaotic energies inside him. The golden light in his eyes dimmed. The pressure in the air lessened.

"Better." Zhang Wei nodded. "Now, we need to prepare. The Regulator will—"

The crack in the sky split open.

White light flooded the temple, so bright that Ren had to shield his eyes. A sound followed—a deep, resonant hum that vibrated through his bones and set his teeth on edge. And then came the *presence*.

It was like standing at the bottom of an ocean trench, with all the weight of the sea pressing down on him. It was like being a child again, lost in a crowd, invisible and insignificant. It was like looking into the eyes of a god and realizing that the god was hungry.

"Too late." Zhang Wei's voice was a whisper. "It's already here."

The light faded. The hum stopped. And in the center of the temple plaza, where the hounds' corpses still lay scattered, stood a figure.

It was humanoid—vaguely—but its proportions were wrong. Too tall. Too thin. Its skin was the color of polished obsidian, and its face had no features except for a single vertical slit where its eyes should have been. It wore no clothes, carried no weapons, but Ren could feel the power radiating from it like heat from a furnace.

**[Regulator Unit: Classification Beta-7]**
**[Purpose: Elemental Deviation Correction]**
**[Current Target: Ren Zhan — Designation: Anomaly]**
**[Action: Immediate Termination]**

The Regulator's head turned toward Ren. The slit on its face opened, revealing a single golden eye that fixed him with a gaze that held no malice, no anger, no emotion at all.

Just purpose.

"Ren." Lin Wei's voice was steady, but he could hear the fear beneath it. "What do we do?"

He looked at the jade disk in his hand. He looked at the Regulator. He looked at the survivors behind him—Lin Wei, Chen Feng, Mei, and the others—people who had survived the Shattering only to face something worse.

"Run," he said. "Take everyone and run."

"What about you?" Mei demanded.

"I'll hold it off."

"That's suicide." Chen Feng's voice was sharp. "You can't fight that thing."

"I know." Ren's hand tightened around the disk. "But I can buy you time. Time to find somewhere safe. Time to figure out how to stop whatever's coming."

The Regulator took a step forward. The stone beneath its foot cracked and crumbled.

"Go." Ren's voice was soft, but it carried. "I'll find you. I promise."

Lin Wei hesitated. For a moment, he thought she would argue. But then she nodded, turned, and started directing the others toward the far end of the plaza.

"Ren." Zhang Wei's voice stopped him. The old man's eyes were fixed on the Regulator. "The disk. It's not just a regulator. It's a key. If you survive this—"

"I know." Ren pressed the disk tighter against his chest. "I'll figure it out."

The Regulator took another step. The ground shook.

Zhang Wei turned and fled, his scarred arm leaving a trail of black smoke in the air.

Ren stood alone in the temple plaza, the jade disk warm against his chest, the golden light flickering at the edges of his vision. The Regulator stopped ten meters away. Its golden eye fixed on him with the patience of something

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Weight of Being First

The golden light faded from Ren's skin, but the *pressure* didn't.

It pressed down on him from all directions, invisible and immense, like being underwater at crushing depth. The temple plaza felt smaller. The sky felt closer. And somewhere beyond the shattered clouds, something was *watching*.

Seventy-one hours. Forty-eight minutes. Eleven seconds.

The timer ticked in his peripheral vision, overlaid on his Status Screen like a countdown to execution.

"Ren." Lin Wei's voice was steady, but her hand trembled as she reached for his arm. "Your eyes."

He blinked. "What about them?"

"Gold," Mei whispered from behind Chen Feng. "They're... they're *glowing* gold."

Ren looked down at his hands. The cuts were gone. The blood had dried, but beneath it, his skin was smooth and unmarked. Not a single wound remained from the hound attack. And yes, when he focused, he could see it—a faint golden luminescence pulsing beneath his skin, like a heartbeat made of light.

*Five Element Resonance. Theoretical state achieved.*

*No. Beyond theoretical.*

*I am the first to exist.*

The thought should have felt triumphant. Instead, it felt like standing on a tightrope over an abyss while someone sawed through the rope behind him.

"You're bleeding," Chen Feng said, pointing at Ren's shoulder.

Ren touched the spot. His fingers came away wet. But when he looked, the wound was already sealing itself, skin knitting together with that same soft glow. The process took three seconds. Maybe four.

*Regeneration. Passive. Elemental combination: Water and Wood.*

The system was teaching him faster now. Throwing information at him like a fire hose. He couldn't process it all, but his *body* could. His cells remembered what his mind hadn't learned yet.

"That's not normal," Lin Wei said flatly. "That's not even *close* to normal."

"I know."

"The guy who cut himself on rebar yesterday is now healing like a—like a—"

"Like something that shouldn't exist," Ren finished. "I know."

The survivors had gathered around him now. Thirteen people. Some clutching makeshift weapons. Others holding each other. All of them looking at Ren like he was either their salvation or their next threat. Maybe both.

A woman in a torn office blazer stepped forward. Her name tag read *Zhang Li*, Sales Department, and her face was streaked with dirt and dried tears. "What happened to you? What did the system do?"

"I activated all five elements," Ren said. "The system didn't plan for it."

"Can you teach us?"

The question hit him like a physical blow. *Teach them.* He looked at their faces—frightened, desperate, hopeful—and felt the weight of their expectations settle onto his shoulders alongside the invisible pressure from above.

"I don't know how," he admitted. "I don't even know what I'm doing. It's instinct. Like breathing."

"Then teach us to breathe," Zhang Li said. "My husband is out there somewhere. My daughter. I need to find them. I need to be *strong enough* to find them."

Ren looked at Lin Wei. She gave a small nod. *They need hope. Give it to them.*

"Okay," he said. "But I'm learning as I go. We all learn together."

---

The next fourteen hours were a blur of trial and error.

Ren discovered that he could *transfer* elemental energy to others—briefly, painfully, with mixed results. Chen Feng could hold a Fire spark for three seconds before it burned out. Lin Wei could channel Water for almost ten. Mei's Wood affinity responded immediately, vines sprouting from her palms within minutes.

But no one could hold two elements at once. No one could combine them.

"You're a bottleneck," Lin Wei said, rubbing her temples. "A living, breathing bottleneck. We can only use what you give us, and you can only give us one at a time."

"Then we need to find others who awakened naturally," Ren said. "There have to be more survivors on other islands."

"The bridges are broken. The gaps are too wide to jump."

"Then we build a bridge."

He said it without thinking. But as the words left his mouth, he felt the elements stir in response. *Earth and Metal. Structural reinforcement. Wood for the frame. Water to bind the mortar. Fire to harden the joints.*

The blueprint appeared in his mind fully formed, like a memory he'd never lived.

*Construction Art: Five Element Bridge (Unranked)*

*Requires: All Five Elements Active*

*Duration: Sustained concentration*

*Warning: High elemental drain. Do not exceed 20 meters.*

"I can do it," Ren said. "I can build us a way across."

---

The first bridge took three hours and nearly killed him.

He stood at the edge of the temple island, staring across a thirty-meter gap to the next floating rock. Below, the void stretched infinitely, dark and hungry. The survivors watched from behind him, their breath held in collective anticipation.

Ren raised his hands.

Earth rose first—stone and concrete from the broken temple, pulled apart and reshaped into a narrow arch. Metal followed, rebar and copper wire from the wreckage weaving through the stone like veins. Wood sprouted from the seams, roots and branches growing at impossible speed, locking everything together. Water filled the gaps, turning to ice in the cracks. Fire swept across the surface, fusing it all into a single seamless structure.

The bridge connected.

And Ren collapsed.

His vision swam. His heart hammered irregularly. The golden light flickered like a dying bulb.

*Elemental exhaustion. Warning: Five Element Drain critical. Rest required: 6 hours.*

"Ren!" Lin Wei caught him before he hit the ground. "You idiot. You used everything."

"Bridge," he managed. "Works."

She looked at the bridge, then back at him. Her eyes were wet. "Yeah. It works."

---

They crossed in groups of three, testing the structure's weight. It held. Ren watched from the ground, too weak to stand, as the survivors made their way across the gap.

*Seventy-one hours became fifty-seven.*

The timer was still ticking.

On the next island, they found bodies. Fifteen of them. Killed not by monsters, but by *people*. The wounds were clean—blade wounds, not claws. And the bodies had been looted.

*Survivors. Armed. Hostile.*

"Stay alert," Chen Feng whispered, gripping his rebar. "They might still be here."

They weren't. But the message was clear: humans were now the predators as much as the monsters.

---

On the third island, they found the first natural awakener.

A boy, maybe sixteen, sitting on a pile of hound corpses with tears streaming down his face. His hands were covered in blood, and when he looked up, electricity crackled between his fingers.

*Element: Metal. Variant: Lightning.*

"Don't come closer," he said, his voice cracking. "I don't want to hurt anyone else."

"You haven't hurt us," Ren said, stepping forward slowly. "We're not your enemies."

"I killed them." The boy gesture at the hounds. "I didn't mean to. I just got *scared* and then—" Lightning arced from his hands, striking a nearby wall and leaving a blackened crater. "I can't control it."

"Yes you can."

"How do you know?"

Ren knelt in front of him. "Because I couldn't control mine either. Not at first. But you're still alive. You're still trying. That's the first step."

The boy stared at him. "Who are you?"

"My name is Ren. And I think we're going to need your help."

---

Forty-three hours remaining.

They'd found seven more awakened survivors across four islands. A woman who could harden her skin to stone. A man who could sense water sources from a kilometer away. A teenager who could make plants grow at will. A child who could talk to birds.

Each one was a piece of a puzzle Ren didn't fully understand.

Each one looked to him for answers he didn't have.

*Why me?* he thought, staring at the false stars that had replaced the real ones. *Why am I the first?*

**SYSTEM MESSAGE**

**Query detected.**

**Answer: Probability alignment. Your elemental frequencies matched the prime resonance threshold during initial system activation.**

**Translation: You were lucky.**

**Correction: You were *designed* to be lucky.**

**The Administrator did not create the Five Element Path.**

**The Administrator *hid* it.**

**You found it anyway.**

**This is why you must be deleted.**

Ren's blood ran cold. *Designed? Hidden? What the hell does that mean?*

But the system offered no further explanation.

---

Thirty-nine hours.

They reached the edge of the island cluster. Beyond it, the void stretched into darkness, broken only by distant floating mountains and the occasional flash of lightning from storms that never ended.

And there, on the far horizon, Ren saw it.

A structure. Massive. Ancient. Impossible.

A tower, rising from a city-sized island, its spire piercing the clouds. And around it, orbiting like moons around a planet, were thousands of smaller islands, each connected to the central mass by bridges of pure light.

"That's not natural," Lin Wei breathed.

"It's not supposed to be here," Ren said. "But it is."

**SYSTEM ALERT**

**Location Discovered: [First Pillar]**

**Description: Anchor point for iteration maintenance.**

**Warning: Administrator forces detected.**

**Estimated defender count: 500+**

**Recommended action: Avoid.**

But even as the message appeared, Ren felt something *pull* at him from the tower. A resonance. A call.

*Five Element Resonance.*

The tower was built for him.

"Ren." Lin Wei grabbed his arm. "Whatever you're thinking—"

"We don't have a choice." He pointed at the timer in his vision. "Thirty-nine hours. Then something comes to delete me. To delete *us*. That tower might have answers. It might have power."

"It might have *death*."

"It definitely has death." Ren looked at her, and for the first time, she saw something other than confusion or fear in his eyes. She saw *certainty*. "But I'm the first. The only. If I die, the path dies with me. If I reach that tower—"

"You might break the system entirely."

"Or I might destroy everything."

The survivors watched him. The awakened boy with lightning in his hands. The woman with stone in her veins. The child who spoke to birds. All of them waiting for his decision.

Ren turned back to the tower.

"Thirty-nine hours," he said. "Let's not waste them."

---

**END CHAPTER 7**

**Status: Ren Zhan**
**Level: 7**
**Elements: All (Active)**
**True Qi Progress: 2.3%**
**Time Until Administrator Arrival: 39 hours, 12 minutes, 44 seconds**
**New Objective: Reach [First Pillar]**
**Warning: Multiple hostile awakened signatures detected on approach path**

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Weight of Attention

The silence that followed the dark message was worse than the screams of the dying.

Ren stood in the center of the plaza, golden light still pulsing beneath his skin, and felt something *watching* him. Not the system. Not the hounds' master. Something older. Something that pressed against his consciousness like a thumb testing a bug's resilience.

"Ren." Lin Wei's voice was careful, measured. "You're still glowing."

He looked at his hands. The golden light had faded to a faint shimmer, barely visible in the moonlight. But he could still feel it—the resonance of five elements humming together in his chest, his blood, his bones. It felt like home. It felt like a target.

Chen Feng stepped forward, rebar raised. Not in defense—in threat. "What the hell was that? The sky went dark. There was a countdown. And now you're standing there like you just became the main character of this nightmare."

"I didn't—"

"Bullshit." Chen Feng's grip tightened. "I saw you kill those things with your bare hands. I saw you *boil one from the inside*. Normal people don't do that. Normal people don't get system messages that talk about 'administrators' and 'deletion'."

The other survivors murmured. Some backed away from Ren. Others edged closer to Chen Feng, seeking the security of a leader who wasn't glowing.

Mei's fire flickered back to life in her palm. "Chen Feng, put the rebar down. He saved us."

"Saved us from what? Hounds that appeared right after he started glowing? You think that's coincidence?"

Ren opened his mouth to argue—then stopped. Chen Feng wasn't wrong. The hounds had come *after* he activated his elements. The system had flagged him *after* he killed them. Cause and effect. Or maybe just correlation. But in a world where everything had turned into a survival game, correlation was enough to get you killed.

"He's not the enemy." Lin Wei stepped between them. "I've known Ren for years. He's a delivery driver. He plays video games. He's not some secret agent or whatever you're imagining."

"People change when they get powers," Chen Feng said. "I've seen it happen twice already. The guy from Building Seven—he got [Fire Manipulation] and burned down his own floor trying to 'purify' it. The woman from the supermarket—she got [Enhanced Strength] and started hoarding all the supplies for herself. Powers change people."

"And what power did you get?" Ren asked quietly.

Chen Feng's jaw tightened. "I didn't get anything. I'm just a guy with a rebar and a survival instinct."

Which meant Chen Feng was jealous. Resentful. He'd watched others awaken while he remained ordinary. And now he was projecting that fear onto the most powerful person he'd seen.

Ren understood. He didn't like it, but he understood.

"Seventy-one hours," Ren said. "That's what the system gave me. Seventy-one hours to reach something called [True Qi] before an 'Administrator' arrives and deletes me. If I'm the threat you think I am, I'll be gone in three days anyway. If I'm not, then we have a bigger problem: something out there is coming to kill me, and I have no idea what it is."

The silence stretched.

Mei's fire dimmed. "True Qi? What's that?"

"I don't know. The system mentioned it in the first message. Something about combining all five elements into a single energy. I thought it was just a theoretical state."

"Wait." Lin Wei's eyes widened. "The Five Element Resonance. That was listed as 'tier unknown' in the system's initial status. It said no human had ever achieved it. But you... you actually did it?"

"Apparently. And apparently it's not supposed to be possible."

Chen Feng lowered the rebar, but his grip didn't loosen. "So what's your plan? Meditate for three days and hope you figure it out?"

"I was thinking more along the lines of trial and error. Kill monsters. Absorb elements. Level up. Whatever it takes."

"And if you fail?"

Ren met his gaze. "Then I get deleted. And something tells me I won't be the only one."

---

The next twelve hours were a blur of violence and discovery.

Ren moved through the island like a predator, hunting the creatures that had once hunted humans. The hounds were the most common—packs of three to five that roamed the ruined streets, feeding on anything that moved. But there were others. Giant insects with chitinous armor. Vines that moved like snakes and wrapped around their prey. A swarm of metallic birds that dove from the sky like living shrapnel.

Each kill brought experience. Each experience brought growth.

**[Elemental Proficiency: Fire +3]**
**[New Skill Unlocked: Flame Step]**
**[Elemental Proficiency: Water +2]**
**[New Skill Unlocked: Blood Boil]**
**[Elemental Proficiency: Earth +4]**
**[New Skill Unlocked: Stone Skin]**

By dawn, Ren had killed forty-seven creatures and gained six levels. His body felt different—lighter, stronger, more attuned to the energies flowing through him. But he still hadn't found the key to True Qi.
